article.title: Kernel code removals driven by LLM-created security reports

summary: The Linux kernel community is actively removing legacy networking code, including drivers for ISA and PCMCIA Ethernet, AX.25 amateur radio protocols, ATM, and ISDN subsystems, largely due to an overwhelming influx of security bug reports generated by large language models (LLMs). This cleanup reflects the challenges of maintaining outdated code with limited volunteer resources and the shifting priorities toward more secure, maintainable kernel code.
keyPoints: Legacy networking subsystems like ISA, PCMCIA Ethernet, AX.25, ATM, and ISDN are being excised from the Linux kernel.
The surge in AI-generated security bug reports has increased maintenance burden, prompting removals to protect developer sanity.
Much of the legacy code lacks active maintenance and fails to meet modern coding and security standards.
Users relying on obsolete hardware are advised to use older kernel versions or rely on enterprise vendors for out-of-tree maintenance.
The removal highlights the impracticality of expecting volunteers to indefinitely support industrial equipment software, especially when manufacturers no longer exist.
Enterprise Linux distributions or consortia may maintain legacy support externally, as upstream kernel focus shifts to current hardware.

rewrite.title: Linux Kernel Prunes Legacy Networking Code Amid AI Bug Surge
rewrite.body:
The Linux kernel development community is undertaking significant removals of legacy networking code, primarily targeting subsystems such as ISA and PCMCIA Ethernet drivers, AX.25 amateur radio protocols, ATM, and ISDN. This initiative is driven by a sharp increase in security bug reports generated by large language models (LLMs), which has overwhelmed maintainers and exposed the challenges of sustaining outdated codebases. The amateur radio stack, notorious as a bug magnet, is among the components being removed to reduce the maintenance burden.

These subsystems have not kept pace with evolving coding standards and security expectations, and their upkeep has largely depended on volunteer efforts. Given that many of these drivers support hardware no longer in active production—often from manufacturers that have ceased operations—the community finds it unreasonable to expect indefinite maintenance without dedicated resources. Consequently, users who still operate on legacy hardware are encouraged to continue using older kernel versions or seek support through enterprise Linux vendors who may maintain such code out of tree.

This shift underscores a broader trend in open-source software maintenance: as AI tools surface more vulnerabilities, the cost of maintaining legacy code rises, prompting a focus on security and maintainability over backward compatibility. While some argue that long-term support distributions or industrial consortia could assume responsibility for these legacy components, the mainline kernel is moving toward shedding obsolete code to streamline development and enhance security.

The removals also raise important considerations about the lifecycle of industrial equipment and the software that supports it, emphasizing the need for users and vendors to plan for software maintenance costs beyond hardware procurement. Ultimately, this pruning reflects a pragmatic response to the realities of modern kernel development in an AI-augmented security landscape.
rewrite.disclaimer: AI-assisted rewrite based on extracted text + discussion signals
